items.question: Explain prior, likelihood, and posterior in a Bayesian model.
items.answer: The prior P(θ) encodes pre-data belief about parameters. The likelihood P(x|θ) scores how probable the observed data is for each θ. The posterior P(θ|x) ∝ P(x|θ)P(θ) is the updated belief after seeing x; divide by P(x) to normalize.

items.question: How does Ridge regression relate to priors?
items.answer: Ridge is MAP estimation for linear models with Gaussian noise and independent Gaussian priors on coefficients. The L2 penalty corresponds to log-prior terms; larger penalty means tighter prior (more shrinkage toward the prior mean).

items.question: Why can a positive medical test still imply low posterior disease probability?
items.answer: If the disease prior is extremely rare, most positives come from false positives despite good specificity. Bayes combines P(+|diseased) and P(+|healthy) with the small prior P(diseased), yielding a modest posterior unless prevalence is higher.
